title: Needle-felted landscape triptych
subtitle: Three small framed landscape panels using layered colour to build sky, hills, and foreground
excerpt: Three A6-sized needle-felted landscape panels worked onto a pre-felt background, using thin layered wisps of roving to build a graduated sky, distant hills, and a foreground colour band. The triptych format gives three chances to develop the layering method on a small scale. Each panel takes 30-45 minutes.
sourceNotes: Pictorial needle-felted landscapes are a contemporary textile art form. The method of building up colour layers on a pre-felt background is observation-led practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A piece of partially felted fabric used as the working surface for pictorial needle felting. The fibres of the background are locked enough to hold applied roving without shifting but not so dense that a needle cannot penetrate easily.
The visual boundary between sky and landscape in a needle-felted landscape panel, created by placing one colour directly above another and softening the edge with fine-needle blending.

content.text: about 15 x 10 cm in a neutral grey or cream. The landscape reads from bottom to top: foreground colour band, mid-ground hills,
, sky. Work bottom to top. Each section is a series of thin layered wisps; depth is created by the layers, not by thick applications.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

items.name: Pre-felt background, approx. 15 x 10 cm, neutral grey or cream
Merino roving in sky colours (pale blue, cream, peach for dawn)
Merino roving in hill and mid-ground colours (grey-green, purple, blue-grey)
Merino roving in foreground colours (dark green, brown, gold for grasses)

content.text: Working from the top edge of the background downward, lay very thin wisps of sky colour across the upper two-thirds of the panel. The sky does not need to be opaque; the neutral background showing through adds depth. Needle lightly with a size 38 needle using vertical strokes across the sky area.

Take a thin wisp of hill colour and lay it in a gentle undulating line about one-third up from the bottom of the panel. Needle it down carefully. The hills should be thinner and paler than the foreground; use pale grey-green or lavender-blue for distant hills. Work additional hills at slightly different levels in varying tones to create depth.

The foreground is the bottom quarter of the panel. Use darker, warmer, or more saturated colours here. Lay short vertical wisps of grass colour, vary the heights slightly, and needle them in with directional downward strokes to suggest grass growing upward. A second layer of a slightly different tone adds texture.

Use the size 40 needle to soften the horizon line between sky and hills, and to define any thin details (tree outlines, reflections in water). Press any loose surface fibres down. Mount in frames once dry.

items.problem: The panel looks flat with no sense of depth
items.cause: All landscape elements are the same colour saturation and value
items.fix: Distant elements must be paler, less saturated, and cooler in tone than foreground elements. Atmospheric perspective: things further away look bluer and lighter.

items.problem: Roving wisps bunch rather than lying flat
items.cause: Too much roving applied at once, or background not firm enough
items.fix: Use the thinnest wisps possible. If the background is soft, needle it a few times to compact it before adding colour.

items.problem: The three panels look too different from each other
items.cause: Colours and proportions varied between panels
items.fix: Pre-mix the same roving for all three panels from the same batch. Measure horizon positions at the same height on each panel.
